common_assets.image_prompt: Studio Ghibli style illustration inspired by a modern startup twist on the classic lion fable: in a glass-walled boardroom, a suited CEO with the head of a lion calmly pulls the biggest slice of a giant pie-chart-shaped cake toward himself while two human co-founders exchange uneasy glances and a tiny intern tries to reach a thin slice at the edge. Warm cinematic lighting, expressive faces, city skyline in the background, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: The largest or most significant portion of something.
en.example_sentence: When the deal closed, the lion-headed CEO slid the biggest slice of the giant cake toward himself, quietly taking the lion's share.

hi.definition: Kisi cheez ka sabse bada aur mukhya bhaag.
hi.example_sentence: Deal final hote hi sher-mukh CEO ne badi se cake ka sabse bada hissa apni taraf khinch liya.

fr.definition: La plus grande partie de quelque chose.
fr.example_sentence: À la fin de l'accord, le PDG à tête de lion a tiré vers lui la plus grosse part du gâteau, s'offrant la part du lion.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Derived from Aesop's Fables, where the lion hunts with partners (often a cow, goat, and sheep) but claims all four quarters of the prey for himself using various excuses like 'I am King' and 'I am strongest'. Originally meant taking the *whole* amount, but shifted in modern English to mean the *majority*.

recent_references.title: The Lion's Share - English Animal Idioms
recent_references.author: Troon Harrison

english_deep_dive.trivia: In the original Latin Phaedrus version, the lion gives four distinct reasons for taking all four parts of the prey.

common_assets.image_prompt: Moody Baroque oil painting of a scene inspired by a famous courtroom‑style TV courtroom drama: a man in a suit, late and dishevelled, stands behind a judge’s bench pointing at a neatly dressed colleague for being late, while a clock in the background clearly shows the time he arrived. Rich textures, dramatic chiaroscuro lighting, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Used to describe criticism that is ironic or hypocritical coming from the person saying it.
en.example_sentence: He quoted the office rules in that courtroom, but it was a bit rich coming from someone who is late every day.

hi.definition: Jab koi khud wahi galti kare jiske liye wo doosron ko tok raha ho.
hi.example_sentence: Usne court‑jaise office mein niyam padhke sabko samay par aane ke liye daanta, lekin uska roz late aana dekhke ye baat bilkul ulta chor kotwal ko daante lagti thi.

fr.definition: Ironique ou hypocrite venant de la part de cette personne.
fr.example_sentence: Il a récité les règles au beau milieu de la réunion, mais c'est un peu fort venant de quelqu'un qui est toujours en retard.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Originated in British colloquialism where 'rich' meant highly seasoned or indigestible food, later metaphorically meaning something hard to swallow (accept). By the 20th century, it solidified as an expression for hypocrisy.

recent_references.title: The Thick of It
recent_references.context: British political satire series where characters frequently retort 'That's a bit rich!' in debates over party hypocrisy and double standards.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Often colloquially shortened to just 'That's rich!' in arguments.

common_assets.image_prompt: Clean Disney Pixar 3D render of a scene representing: Opening the investigation file turned out to be opening a of corruption.. The scene should capture the essence of 'Pandora's box'. High quality, detailed, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: A process that generates many complicated problems as the result of unwise interference.
en.example_sentence: Opening the investigation file turned out to be opening a Pandora's box of corruption.

hi.definition: Ek aisi prakriya jo anjaane mein dher saari nayi samasyaein paida kar deti hai.
hi.example_sentence: Jaanch ki file kholna bhrashtachaar ka ek naya musibaton ka pitara kholne jaisa nikla.

fr.definition: Source de nombreux problèmes imprévus.
fr.example_sentence: Rouvrir ce dossier s'est révélé être l'ouverture d'une boîte de Pandore de corruption.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Greek mythology. Pandora, the first woman, was given a jar (later mistranslated as a 'box' by Erasmus in the 16th century) containing all the evils of the world. She opened it out of curiosity, releasing them, leaving only Hope inside.
english_deep_dive.trivia: The 'box' was originally a 'pithos' (large storage jar) in Hesiod's original Greek text, not a box.

common_assets.image_prompt: Classical Greek marble statue style of a scene inspired by a modern protest‑rally poster: a speaker on a stone podium passionately addresses a crowd of activists already holding identical banners about the same cause, with a small, skeptical outsider barely visible at the edge of the square, looking like he might be the only one not convinced. Warm, sculptural lighting, visible dust on the steps, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: To try to persuade people who already agree with you.
en.example_sentence: He delivered his big speech to the crowd holding the same banners, clearly preaching to the choir.

hi.definition: Un logon ko samjhaana jo pehle se hi aapki baat se sehmat hain.
hi.example_sentence: Usne unhi karyakartaon ko bhashan diya jo pahle se hi unke jaisi tole bicha rahe the, machhli ke paas paani dikhane jaisa tha.

fr.definition: Essayer de convaincre des personnes déjà d'accord.
fr.example_sentence: Ses grands mots résonnaient devant une foule de militants levant déjà les mêmes bannières : il ne faisait que prêcher des convertis.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Originated in the US in the 1970s (derived from the earlier 'preaching to the converted' from 1867). It refers to a minister preaching to the church choir members, who are already the most committed believers. Over time it migrated into politics and activism to describe speeches aimed at ones own side rather than a genuinely opposing audience.

recent_references.title: In Praise of Preaching to the Choir
recent_references.author: Mother Jones
recent_references.context: Long-form political commentary that defends the strategic value of rallying one’s own base, even when the audience is already aligned, rather than trying to win over opponents.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Often used in politics when candidates hold rallies in their own strongholds, where the crowd is mostly sympathetic and unlikely to change its mind.

common_assets.image_prompt: Da Vinci style sepia sketch of a scene representing: students were burning the midnight oil to finish their thesis before the deadline.. The scene should capture the essence of 'Burning the midnight oil'. High quality, detailed, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: To work late into the night.
en.example_sentence: The students were burning the midnight oil to finish their thesis before the deadline.

hi.definition: Der raat tak kaam karna ya padhai karna.
hi.example_sentence: Chhatra deadline se pehle thesis khatam karne ke liye raat din ek kar rahe the.

fr.definition: Travailler très tard la nuit.
fr.example_sentence: Les étudiants travaillaient tard dans la nuit pour finir leur thèse avant l'échéance.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Dates back to the 17th century (first cited by Francis Quarles, 1635) when working late literally required burning oil in a lamp. The phrase survived the invention of electricity as a metaphor for diligent late-night effort.
english_deep_dive.trivia: Dolly Parton released a collaborative album titled 'Burning the Midnight Oil' in 1972.

common_assets.image_prompt: High-contrast Death Note anime style of a scene representing: media frenzy over the celebrity's typo was just a storm in a teacup.. The scene should capture the essence of 'Storm in a teacup'. High quality, detailed, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Great outrage or excitement about a trivial matter.
en.example_sentence: The media frenzy over the celebrity's typo was just a storm in a teacup.

hi.definition: Chhoti si baat par bahut bada hungama karna.
hi.example_sentence: Celebrity ki spelling mistake par media ka hungama bas baat ka batangad tha.

fr.word_display: Une tempête dans un verre d'eau
fr.word_native: Une tempête dans un verre d'eau
fr.definition: Beaucoup de bruit pour rien.
fr.example_sentence: La frénésie médiatique autour de la coquille de la célébrité n'était qu'une tempête dans un verre d'eau.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: First recorded in 1838 by Scottish novelist Catherine Sinclair. It likely evolved from the Latin phrase 'excitare fluctus in simpulo' (stirring up billows in a ladle) used by Cicero.
english_deep_dive.trivia: Americans typically use the variation 'Tempest in a teapot'.

common_assets.image_prompt: Dreamy Surrealist Salvador Dali style of a scene inspired by a famous travel‑magazine cover: a jaw‑dropping mountain valley at sunrise, with tourists frozen in awe on a viewpoint ledge, one literally dropping their camera, while unnaturally long, soft reflections of the peaks stretch across a mirror‑calm lake. Hyper‑detailed, soft borders, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Having great physical beauty and appeal.
en.example_sentence: The pulchritudinous valley at sunrise left the tourists standing in stunned silence.

hi.definition: Bahut adhik khoobsurat ya aakarshak.
hi.example_sentence: Suraj uge peddon par aayi pahadi wadiyon ka ati sundar drishya paryatakon ko bina shabd bana kar rakh diya.

fr.definition: D'une beauté physique exceptionnelle.
fr.example_sentence: Le paysage d'une grande beauté au lever du soleil a laissé les touristes figés, sans voix.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Latin 'pulchritudo' (beauty). While it existed in Middle English, it vanished from British usage in the 17th century, only to be revived in American English in the late 19th century, often used somewhat humorously or grandiloquently.

recent_references.title: The Most Beautiful Places in the World
recent_references.author: National Geographic
recent_references.context: Descriptive travel feature that uses elevated, almost essayistic language to portray landscapes so stunning they are compared to 'pulchritudinous' scenes from dreams.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Despite meaning 'beautiful', the word itself is often considered ugly‑sounding due to its hard consonants.

common_assets.image_prompt: Vibrant Cyberpunk Neon style of a scene inspired by a famous tech‑firm boardroom in a sci‑fi film: a young analyst in a hoodie points at a holographic dashboard where subtle warning patterns glow red, while older executives around the table stare in shock, and a digital ticker behind them ticks down the millions they’ve just saved. High contrast neon grid, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Having a ready insight into and understanding of things.
en.example_sentence: Her perspicacious reading of the holographic trends saved the company millions before the crisis hit.

hi.definition: Jo cheezon ko jaldi aur gehraai se samajh le.
hi.example_sentence: Usne holo‑screen ke pattar padhte hue, aane wale sankat se pehle hi company ke karodon bacha diye—sach‑me uski teevra‑buddhi thi.

fr.definition: Qui a de la pénétration d'esprit, qui voit ce qui échappe aux autres.
fr.example_sentence: Son regard perspicace sur les tendances holographiques a permis d'économiser des millions à l'entreprise avant l'effondrement.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Latin 'perspicax' (sharp‑sighted), from 'perspicere' (to look through). It implies the ability to see through confusion to the truth, much like a keen analyst spotting a hidden pattern in noisy data.

recent_references.title: Why Some Analysts Seem to See the Future First
recent_references.author: The Economist
recent_references.context: Feature on financial and tech analysts whose 'perspicacious' reads of market signals help their firms avoid billion‑dollar mistakes.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Often confused with 'perspicuous', which means clearly expressed (easy to understand), whereas 'perspicacious' means having keen understanding.

common_assets.image_prompt: Soft Watercolor painting of a scene inspired by a classic university lecture-hall poster: a professor in tweed stands at a chalkboard covered with long, looping words, while a row of freshmen sit with blank, glazed eyes, one student circling a word like 'incongruence' in a notebook with a tiny question mark. Soft light from high windows, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Characterized by very long words; long‑winded.
en.example_sentence: His sesquipedalian explanation of the simplest rule left the freshmen staring at the board in confusion.

hi.definition: Bahut lambe aur mushkil shabdon ka prayog karne wala.
hi.example_sentence: Sabse aasan niyam ko samjhate hue uski lambe‑chaude shabdon wali bhaashan ne naye chhatron ko bilkul bhaat kar diya.

fr.definition: Qui utilise des mots très longs et savants.
fr.example_sentence: Son explication sesquipédalienne de la règle la plus simple a laissé les étudiants de première année figés devant le tableau.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Coined by the Roman poet Horace in 'Ars Poetica' as 'sesquipedalia verba', literally meaning 'words a foot and a half long'. It was used to criticize poets who used big words to sound impressive.

recent_references.title: Why Some Writers Obsess Over Long Words
recent_references.author: The Atlantic
recent_references.context: Essay on academic and political language that playfully calls out 'sesquipedalian loquaciousness' in modern speeches and textbooks.

english_deep_dive.trivia: The fear of long words is ironically named 'Hippopotomonstrosesquipedaliophobia'.

common_assets.image_prompt: Bold Lino Cut graphic style of a scene inspired by a famous film’s funeral‑scene poster: a small church interior at dusk, mourners in dark coats, a violinist in the corner drawing a slow bow, while a single candle flickers on the coffin; heavy shadows stretch across the tiles and everybody’s shoulders slump under the weight of the lugubrious music. High contrast, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Looking or sounding sad and dismal.
en.example_sentence: The lugubrious music at the funeral made even the stoic mourners’ eyes glisten.

hi.definition: Jo dikhne ya sunne mein bahut udaas aur dukhi lage.
hi.example_sentence: Antim sanskar ki shok‑poorn dhun ne sabki aankhon se aansu nikaal diye, jaise har raag udaasi ka hi thaa.

fr.definition: D'une tristesse accablante et sombre.
fr.example_sentence: La musique lugubre des funérailles a fait briller les yeux des plus stoïques parmi les pleureurs.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Latin 'lugere' (to mourn). It entered English in the early 17th century. It has evolved to often imply an exaggerated or performative gloominess in modern literature, like a character’s drawn‑out sigh or a film score that lingers on minor chords long after the moment is over.

recent_references.title: Why Modern Soundtracks Love the Lugubrious Minor Chord
recent_references.author: Wired
recent_references.context: Deep‑dive on how contemporary film and gaming music often uses lugubrious, slow‑moving minor progressions to heighten emotional scenes without dialogue.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Often used in Gothic fiction to set a somber mood, where the atmosphere itself feels like a character that never stops mourning.

common_assets.image_prompt: Studio Ghibli style illustration of a scene inspired by a famous school‑library scene in a teen drama: in a sunlit reading room, a group of teenagers in hoodies and headphones laugh, toss a crumpled paper airplane, and bang a chair, while a librarian with a stern look raises a finger as two others bury their heads in books trying to ignore the chaos. Warm pastels, soft shadows, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Noisy and difficult to control.
en.example_sentence: The obstreperous teenagers in the library made the librarian motion them toward the door.

hi.definition: Shor machane wala aur kabu mein na aane wala.
hi.example_sentence: Library ke andar shor machate upadravi kishoron ko librarian ne aangan ki taraf ishara karke bhej diya.

fr.definition: Bruyant et difficile à contrôler.
fr.example_sentence: Les adolescents turbulents dans la bibliothèque ont fait signe au bibliothécaire de se diriger vers la porte.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Latin 'obstreperus' (clamorous), formed from 'ob' (against) + 'strepere' (to make a noise). It implies actively shouting *against* restraint, like a noisy crowd refusing to quiet down or a classroom that refuses to listen to the teacher.

recent_references.title: When Classrooms Turn Into Little Thunderstorms
recent_references.author: The Guardian
recent_references.context: Feature on modern school culture where ‘obstreperous’, high‑energy students both challenge and reshape classroom dynamics in digital‑heavy learning environments.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Often used to describe unruly children, but historically used for political mobs and boisterous young men in the 18th century resisting authority.

common_assets.image_prompt: Moody Baroque oil painting of a scene inspired by a famous corporate‑office satire: at a long wooden desk, a manager taps a thick, scroll‑like email on the screen while coworkers yawn, one buries their head in their hands and another glances at the clock, clearly dreading the next endless memo. Warm candle‑like lighting, visible paragraphs of text on the screen, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: (Of speech or writing) using or containing too many words; tediously lengthy.
en.example_sentence: His prolix emails ate up the whole screen, so the team quietly hit ‘archive’ instead of reading them.

hi.definition: Jarurat se jyada shabdon ka istemal karne wala.
hi.example_sentence: Jab uska vistaar‑poorn email khula to har kisi ne screen se dheem‑dheem se aankhein bana li, aur kisi ne bhi pura padhne ki himmat nahi dikhayi.

fr.definition: Qui est trop long et bavard dans ses écrits ou ses paroles.
fr.example_sentence: Ses courriels prolixes prenaient toute la fenêtre, et toute l’équipe fermait tranquillement l’onglet sans les lire.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Latin 'prolixus' meaning 'poured forth' or 'extended', from *pro* 'forth' + *liquere* 'to flow'. The image is of words gushing forward like a stream, often past the point where they are still interesting. It entered English via Old French and has kept its slightly formal, slightly negative tone ever since.

recent_references.title: Why So Many Work Emails Sound Like a Novel
recent_references.author: Bloomberg Businessweek
recent_references.context: Investigation into modern corporate communication, noting how 'prolix' long‑paragraph emails and memos have become a major source of productivity drain and digital fatigue.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Unlike 'loquacious', which can sound charming or witty, 'prolix' almost always points toward boredom or frustration with someone who just will not stop writing.

common_assets.image_prompt: Clean Disney Pixar 3D render of a scene inspired by a famous late‑night‑scrolling meme: a young woman sits up in bed bathed in cold blue light from her phone, endlessly pulling down a dark‑mode news feed filled with alarm‑like headlines while a tiny digital clock on the bedside table ticks past 3 AM and a sleeping cat curls away from the glow. Warm‑cool contrast, soft shadows, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: The act of spending an excessive amount of time consuming negative news online.
en.example_sentence: By 3 AM the screen was still glowing, and she realized she had been doomscrolling for hours.

hi.definition: Internet par lagatar buri khabrein padhne ki lat.
hi.example_sentence: Jab ghadi ne 3 bajate dikhaye tou usne samjha ki wo puri raat dukh‑bhari scrolling kar rahi thi.

fr.definition: Consommer compulsivement des mauvaises nouvelles en ligne.
fr.example_sentence: Vers 3 heures du matin, l'écran était encore allumé : elle s'était perdue en défilement morbide pendant des heures.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Coined on social media around 2018, 'doomscrolling' fuses 'doom' (a sense of inescapable fate) with the modern interface gesture of scrolling endlessly through feeds. It exploded in usage during the COVID‑19 pandemic, when people stayed up late tracking grim headlines on their phones instead of sleeping.

recent_references.title: Doomscrolling Dangers: Why Bad News Keeps Us Stuck Online
recent_references.author: Harvard Health Publishing
recent_references.context: Medical‑style feature on how compulsive doomscrolling is linked to anxiety, insomnia, and lower life satisfaction, with advice for breaking the cycle.

english_deep_dive.trivia: The term was highlighted by Oxford Languages as a standout word of 2020, and the World Health Organization later linked such behavior to the 'infodemic'‑style anxiety that comes from constant exposure to crisis‑related news.

common_assets.image_prompt: Studio Ghibli style illustration inspired by a Gen Z romance anime: two young people sit side by side on a rooftop at dusk, shoulders almost touching, sharing earphones from one phone; one leans in slightly with a soft smile, the other stares at the city skyline with a wistful, uncertain expression. On the phone screen between them, a chat window shows three dots blinking but no message sent. Warm sunset gradient, neon city glow below, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: A romantic or sexual relationship that is not considered to be formal or established.
en.example_sentence: TThey shared earphones on that rooftop every evening, never once naming what they were — the classic situationship, more than friends but too afraid to say more.

hi.definition: Ek aisa rishta jo dosti se jyada ho par poori tarah se prem-sambandh na ho.
hi.example_sentence: Har shaam uss chhat par ek doosre ke kareeb baithe earphones baantte the, lekin kabhi kuch naam nahi diya — wahi kachcha rishta, doston se jyada, par zyada kehne ki himmat nahi.

fr.definition: Une relation romantique sans étiquette claire ou engagement.
fr.example_sentence: Chaque soir sur ce toit, ils partageaient les mêmes écouteurs sans jamais mettre un mot sur ce qu'ils vivaient — une vraie relation floue, plus que des amis, mais trop hésitants pour dire davantage.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: A blend of 'situation' and 'relationship', the word emerged in the late 2000s dating blogosphere but exploded in usage around 2020–2022 to describe the Gen Z preference for low-pressure romantic grey zones. Tinder's 'Year in Swipe' report for 2022 recorded a 49% spike in users adding it to their profiles, cementing it as a mainstream relationship status.

recent_references.title: Situationships: Why Gen Z are embracing the grey area
recent_references.author: BBC Worklife
recent_references.context: Widely cited feature exploring how Gen Z is actively choosing the undefined middle ground between casual and committed, drawing on sociology research from the University of Michigan.

recent_references.title: Ode to a Gen‑Z Situationship
recent_references.author: The New York Times (Modern Love)
recent_references.context: Personal essay in the NYT's celebrated Modern Love column capturing the emotional complexity and quiet longing of a situationship between two people unwilling to define what they have.

english_deep_dive.trivia: In 2023, 'situationship' was a finalist for Oxford's Word of the Year, ultimately losing to 'rizz'. Oxford noted its usage had been 'growing steadily in frequency', showing the term had deeply resonated with modern daters.

common_assets.image_prompt: Studio Ghibli style illustration inspired by a psychological thriller anime: a young woman stands in a long Victorian corridor holding a lit candle, staring at a wall painting whose frame is visibly crooked; behind her, a tall shadowy figure in a suit straightens his tie and says with a calm smile 'nothing has changed', while the gas lamps lining the corridor flicker — some brighter, some dimmer — casting her face half in warm gold and half in cold shadow, making the entire hallway feel subtly wrong. Expressive eyes, intricate wallpaper detail, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Psychologically manipulating someone into questioning their own perception, memory, or sanity.
en.example_sentence: She stood in the corridor certain the painting had moved, but his calm smile insisted she was imagining it — gaslighting her right under the flickering lamps.

hi.definition: Kisi ke dimaag ke saath khelna taaki wo apni hi soch ya yaadasht par shaque kare.
hi.example_sentence: Wo guliyara mein khadi thi, yakin tha ki tasveer hili hai, lekin uski shant muskaan kehti rahi ki sab uska wahm hai — roshni ke kaampte saaye mein woh use bhramit karta raha.

fr.definition: Manipulation mentale qui pousse quelqu'un à douter de sa propre perception ou de sa mémoire.
fr.example_sentence: Elle était sûre que le tableau avait bougé, mais son sourire calme persistait à lui dire que c'était son imagination — la gaslightant sous les lampes vacillantes du couloir.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: The term comes from the 1938 play 'Gas Light' and its famous 1940s film adaptations, where a husband secretly dims the gas lights and then insists to his wife that nothing has changed, making her believe she is going insane. Over time, the story became a cultural shorthand for intimate‑partner manipulation, and by the 21st century 'gaslighting' expanded into everyday language to describe patterns of psychological abuse in relationships, workplaces, and even politics.

recent_references.title: Word of the Year: 'Gaslighting'
recent_references.author: Merriam-Webster
recent_references.context: An in‑depth feature explaining how 'gaslighting' moved from the plot of an old thriller into a widely used term for modern emotional and psychological abuse.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Searches for 'gaslighting' surged so sharply that it was named Word of the Year by a major English dictionary in 2022, reflecting growing public awareness of subtle forms of coercive control.

common_assets.image_prompt: High-contrast Death Note anime style illustration: a young salaried worker sits at a fluorescent-lit office desk at exactly 5:00 PM, calmly closing his laptop with a faint smile while a glowing stack of unread emails pulses on his screen; behind him, a stressed manager rushes in waving papers, tie loosened, but the worker is already sliding on his jacket and reaching for his bag, completely unbothered. The clock on the wall is frozen at 5:00. Dark sharp shadows, dramatic office lighting, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Doing the absolute minimum requirements of one's job and putting in no more time or effort than strictly necessary.
en.example_sentence: At exactly 5 PM he closed his laptop and reached for his jacket — the emails could wait. That was quiet quitting in its purest form.

hi.definition: Naukri mein bas utna hi karna jitna zaroori ho, usse zyada bilkul nahi.
hi.example_sentence: Theek 5 baje usne laptop band kiya aur jacket uthaa li — baaqi emails kal dekhenge. Yahi hai asli simit kaam ki bhavna.

fr.definition: Faire le strict minimum au travail sans s'engager émotionnellement.
fr.example_sentence: À 17 h pile, il a fermé son ordinateur et attrapé sa veste — les e-mails pouvaient attendre. C'était la démission silencieuse dans toute sa splendeur.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Popularized by a viral TikTok by Zaid Khan in July 2022, 'quiet quitting' reframes chronic overwork as a choice rather than a virtue. It does not mean literally quitting — it means quietly withdrawing the unpaid emotional and physical labour that hustle culture had normalised as mandatory.

recent_references.title: Employee Burnout is Costing the World More Than We Think
recent_references.author: Times of India
recent_references.context: Report citing Gallup data showing 82% of employees are at risk of burnout in 2025, with Gen Z hitting peak burnout at just 25 years old — the structural backdrop that makes quiet quitting a rational response rather than laziness.

recent_references.title: Gravitas: What's Quiet Quitting?
recent_references.author: WION
recent_references.context: One of the earliest mainstream broadcast explainers on the trend, framing quiet quitting as 'quitting the idea of going above and beyond your KRA', and sparking a global workplace conversation.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Critics argue it is just a new name for 'work-to-rule' — a formal labour union tactic first recorded in 1920, where workers slow output by following rules to the letter. The irony: what unions once used as a protest weapon, Gen Z independently rediscovered as a mental health boundary.

common_assets.image_prompt: Soft Watercolor painting inspired by a famous university debate hall: a young woman stands at a carved wooden podium under a single warm spotlight, one hand raised mid-gesture, eyes locked on the crowd; in the audience, three rows of listeners lean forward — one dabbing their eye with a sleeve, another gripping the armrest, a third frozen mid-applause. The hall glows amber, the speaker's words rendered as faint golden ripples in the air between her and the audience. High quality, detailed, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing.
en.example_sentence: Her eloquent speech moved the audience to tears.

hi.definition: Jo bolne ya likhne mein bahut prabhavshali ho.
hi.example_sentence: Uske bhavpurn bhaashan ne darshakon ki aankhon mein aansu la diye.

fr.definition: Qui s'exprime avec aisance et force de persuasion.
fr.example_sentence: Son discours éloquent a ému le public aux larmes.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Latin 'eloquentia', from 'eloqui' (to speak out). Historically associated with orators like Cicero.

recent_references.title: Oxford Union Address
recent_references.author: Shashi Tharoor
recent_references.context: Tharoor's now-legendary speech on Britain's colonial legacy in India is widely cited as a masterclass in modern eloquence — combining scholarly precision with emotional force and rhetorical timing, and accumulating over five million YouTube views.

recent_references.title: Why Great Oratory Still Matters in the Age of Algorithms
recent_references.author: The Atlantic
recent_references.context: Feature arguing that in a world of short-form content and algorithmic feeds, genuinely eloquent long-form speech — the kind that builds a sustained emotional argument — has become rarer and therefore more powerful.

english_deep_dive.trivia: In informal British English, a naturally eloquent person is said to have 'the gift of the gab' — an ironic, almost suspicious compliment implying their smoothness comes a little too easily.

common_assets.image_prompt: Studio Ghibli style illustration inspired by a classic election-rally scene: a suited politician stands at a giant podium beneath floodlights, arms wide open, mouth mid-roar, while giant golden words like 'GLORY', 'PROMISE', 'FUTURE' float theatrically above the crowd like animated banners; in the foreground, two citizens squint at each other — one holds a notepad with the words 'But... the plan?' scrawled on it, finding nothing but empty slogans. Warm amber crowd lighting, expressive faces, dramatic sky backdrop, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: The art of effective or persuasive speaking or writing, often often implied to be lacking sincerity.
en.example_sentence: The politician's speech was full of fiery rhetoric but short on actual plans.

hi.definition: Prabhavshali bolne ki kala, aksar jisme sachchayi kam ho.
hi.example_sentence: Neta ka bhaashan joshile alankaarik shabdon se bhara tha par usme asli yojanaein kam thi.

fr.definition: L'art de bien parler, souvent avec une nuance de manque de sincérité.
fr.example_sentence: Le discours du politicien était plein de rhétorique enflammée mais manquait de plans réels.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Greek 'rhetor' (public speaker), rhetoric was defined by Aristotle in his treatise 'Rhetoric' as 'the faculty of observing in any given case the available means of persuasion'. For the Greeks and Romans, it was a noble civic art — Cicero and Demosthenes were its heroes. By the Renaissance, it was one of the three pillars of a classical education, alongside grammar and logic. In modern times, often pejorative ('empty rhetoric').

recent_references.title: How We Entered the New Age of Political Rhetoric
recent_references.author: The Guardian
recent_references.context: A sharp analysis on how democracies are increasingly prioritising rhetoric over the less visible but crucial functions of governance — arguing that the shift away from technocratic politics risks a system that talks loudly but delivers little.

recent_references.title: US Political Rhetoric: Analysis of 8 Million Speeches
recent_references.author: Phys.org / Garcia et al.
recent_references.context: A landmark cross-national study examining Congressional speeches from 1879–2022, finding that the decline of evidence-based rhetoric since the 1970s closely tracks with rising political polarisation, lower legislative productivity, and growing inequality.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Aristotle identified three branches of rhetoric: Ethos (credibility of the speaker), Pathos (emotional appeal), and Logos (logical argument). Modern political speechwriters still use all three — though critics argue the balance has shifted dangerously toward Pathos alone.

common_assets.image_prompt: Studio Ghibli style illustration inspired by a famous art-restoration scene: a young woman leans inches away from a giant oil painting on a museum wall, squinting at a barely-there difference in two almost-identical shades of blue in the sky; behind her, two curators argue loudly pointing at the whole canvas, completely missing the tiny detail she alone has spotted. Warm amber gallery lighting, intricate brushstroke texture, magnifying glass catching the light, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: A subtle difference in or shade of meaning, expression, or sound.
en.example_sentence: While the curators argued about the whole painting, she leaned in closer — it was the nuance of two almost-identical blues that told the real story.

hi.definition: Kisi cheez, arth ya bhav ka bahut chhota aur sookshm antar.
hi.example_sentence: Jab sablog poori painting ke baare mein jhagad rahe the, usne paas jaake dekha — do lagbhag ek jaisi neeli rangon ki bariki ne poori kahani bata di.

fr.definition: Une différence subtile ou une nuance de sens, d'expression ou de son.
fr.example_sentence: Pendant que les conservateurs débattaient de l'ensemble du tableau, elle s'approcha — c'était la nuance entre deux bleus presque identiques qui racontait la vraie histoire.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Borrowed from French 'nuance' (shade, hue), from the verb 'nuer' (to shade colours), itself rooted in Latin 'nubes' (cloud) — evoking the barely perceptible way clouds shift tone across a sky. It entered English in the 18th century referring first to colour gradation, then to subtle shades of meaning or feeling. By the 20th century it had become an indispensable term in literary criticism, music theory, and diplomacy — anywhere that the gap between two almost-identical things carries enormous weight.

recent_references.title: The Nuance of the Word 'Nuance'
recent_references.author: The Stanford Daily
recent_references.context: A widely cited campus essay noting the explosive spike in use of 'nuance' in academic and intellectual discourse, where it had become a signal of sophisticated thinking — and sometimes a buzzword deployed to avoid taking a clear position.

recent_references.title: Sense and Sensibility
recent_references.author: Jane Austen
recent_references.context: Frequently cited by literary scholars as one of the finest examples of nuance in the English novel — where the difference between two sisters' almost-identical emotional situations carries the entire moral weight of the story.

english_deep_dive.trivia: The word's Latin root 'nubes' (cloud) is a perfect accidental metaphor — just as clouds shift tone imperceptibly across a sky, nuance describes differences so small that most people walk right past them.

common_assets.image_prompt: Moody Baroque oil painting inspired by a dramatic scientific-discovery scene: in a candlelit laboratory, a young scientist holds up a glowing double-helix model toward the light while three older professors in the background stare at their dusty chalkboards full of now-obsolete diagrams, one visibly erasing his own work with a stunned expression. The glowing helix casts new light across everything in the room, making the old equations look faded and wrong. Rich chiaroscuro lighting, dramatic shadows, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: A typical example or pattern of something; a model.
en.example_sentence: When she held up the double helix, the older professors began quietly erasing their chalkboards — the discovery had shifted the entire paradigm.

hi.definition: Kisi cheez ka ek vishishth udaharan ya namuna.
hi.example_sentence: Jab usne double helix utha ke dikhaya, bade professors ne chupchap apne chalkboard mita diye — uss khoj ne poora pratiman badal diya tha.

fr.definition: Un exemple typique ou un modèle de quelque chose.
fr.example_sentence: Quand elle a soulevé la double hélice, les vieux professeurs ont commencé à effacer leurs tableaux en silence — la découverte avait fait basculer tout le paradigme.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Greek 'paradeigma' (pattern, model), the word existed in English for centuries in its grammatical sense — a paradigm was simply a table of verb conjugations. It was American philosopher and physicist Thomas Kuhn who radically expanded its meaning in his 1962 book 'The Structure of Scientific Revolutions', defining a scientific paradigm as the entire worldview, methods, and assumptions that a scientific community accepts as self-evident — until anomalies accumulate and a crisis forces a 'paradigm shift'.

recent_references.title: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
recent_references.author: Thomas Kuhn
recent_references.context: The single book most responsible for giving 'paradigm' its modern meaning. Kuhn argued that science does not progress linearly but through periodic revolutions where an old paradigm collapses under the weight of unexplained anomalies and is replaced by a new one — as happened when Copernicus replaced geocentrism, or when DNA replaced protein-based theories of heredity.

recent_references.title: What Is a Paradigm? — Thomas Kuhn Explained
recent_references.author: The Living Philosophy
recent_references.context: A widely read modern explainer on Kuhn's framework, noting that the word 'paradigm' now appears across business strategy, tech, and culture — often stripped of its original precision, becoming a vague synonym for 'big change'.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Kuhn himself later admitted he used the word 'paradigm' in at least 21 different ways throughout his own book — an irony not lost on philosophers of science, since the book that gave the word its modern precision was itself accused of lacking it.

common_assets.image_prompt: Clean Disney Pixar 3D render inspired by a famous corporate office satire: a bird's-eye view of an open-plan office floor split down the middle by a tall transparent glass wall; on one side, the Marketing team celebrates around a campaign board, and on the other, the Engineering team stares at code screens — both solving the exact same problem but unable to see each other's solution. A single sticky note on the glass from one side reads 'Help?' but nobody on the other side has noticed. Warm-cool colour contrast across the divide, expressive characters, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Kept in isolation in a way that hinders communication and cooperation.
en.example_sentence: The two teams were solving the same problem on opposite sides of a glass wall — so siloed that neither noticed the other's 'Help?' note.

hi.definition: Aise alag rakha jana ki aapsi baatcheet aur sahyog mein badha aaye.
hi.example_sentence: Dono teams ek hi samasya ek kaanch ki deewar ke doosri taraf hal kar rahi thi — itni vibhagit ki kisi ne doosre ka 'Help?' note nahi dekha.

fr.definition: Isolé de manière à empêcher la communication et la coopération.
fr.example_sentence: Les deux équipes résolvaient le même problème de chaque côté d'une paroi en verre — si cloisonnées que personne n'avait remarqué le mot 'Aide ?' de l'autre côté.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: The metaphor is drawn from grain silos — the tall cylindrical towers on farms that keep different grains strictly separate to prevent contamination. Applied to business management in the 1980s to describe departments that hoard information, protect their own turf, and fail to communicate across boundaries. What began as a structural observation became a diagnosis: siloed organisations don't just waste effort, they create institutional blindness, where critical knowledge sits unused in one corner of the building while another team desperately searches for it.

recent_references.title: The Silo Effect: The Peril of Expertise and the Promise of Breaking Down Barriers
recent_references.author: Gillian Tett
recent_references.context: Financial Times editor and anthropologist Gillian Tett argues that silos — in banks, governments, and tech companies — are not accidents but products of how humans classify and protect their own worlds. She traces how siloed thinking directly contributed to the 2008 financial crisis, where departments within the same bank had no idea what the others were doing.

recent_references.title: What Causes Silos in Organizations?
recent_references.author: Privilege Press
recent_references.context: A sharp analysis noting that silos rarely arrive with fanfare — they form quietly, as a byproduct of decisions that once seemed sensible, and thrive precisely because 'the cost of isolation is hidden while the cost of collaboration is visible and scheduled'.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Gillian Tett's 'The Silo Effect' (2015) argues that silos cause institutional blindness — but her most striking case study is Sony, which had the technology to build the iPod years before Apple but couldn't, because its music division and electronics division were too siloed to work together.

common_assets.image_prompt: Classical Greek marble statue style of a scene inspired by a famous space-age announcement: a young visionary stands before a giant projection of a red planet, pointing at it with one arm outstretched while a room full of scientists and journalists stare back — some scribbling furiously, some slack-jawed, and one older professor in the front row has dropped his pen entirely. The posture and composition echo the classical hero pose, rendered in cool marble tones with dramatic uplighting. High quality, detailed, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Showing a willingness to take surprisingly bold risks.
en.example_sentence: The audacious plan to colonize Mars stunned the scientific community. He pointed at the red planet on the screen and named a launch date — an audacious move that made the professor in the front row drop his pen.

hi.definition: Hairan kar dene wala jokhim uthane ki himmat dikhana.
hi.example_sentence: Usne screen par laal grah ki taraf ungli uthaai aur launch ki taareekh bata di — itna sahasi kadam ki aage baithe professor ke haath se pen gir gaya.

fr.definition: Montrant une volonté de prendre des risques audacieux.
fr.example_sentence: Le plan audacieux de coloniser Mars a stupéfié la communauté scientifique. Il a pointé la planète rouge sur l'écran et annoncé une date de lancement — un geste si audacieux que le professeur au premier rang a lâché son stylo.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Latin 'audax' (bold), itself from 'audere' (to dare) — literally meaning 'one who advances forward'. The word entered English in the mid-16th century and has always carried a double edge: audacity is admired in heroes and condemned in villains. The same act — ignoring the rules, defying the consensus — is audacious when it succeeds and reckless when it fails. Barack Obama leaned into the positive pole with his 2006 book 'The Audacity of Hope', cementing the word in modern political aspiration. Elon Musk's announcements of Mars colonisation timelines then handed it a new home in the vocabulary of tech ambition.

recent_references.title: The Audacity of Hope
recent_references.author: Barack Obama
recent_references.context: Obama's pre-presidential book borrowed the phrase from a Reverend Jeremiah Wright sermon, arguing that hope itself — in the face of cynicism and impossibility — is an audacious act. The title single-handedly made 'audacity' a word of political aspiration rather than arrogance for a generation.

recent_references.title: Audacious Visions of the Total Conquest of Space
recent_references.author: Vocabulary.com / various citations
recent_references.context: A widely cited usage note tracing how 'audacious' has become the go-to adjective in science and technology journalism for plans that break with consensus — from space colonisation to AI breakthroughs — where the line between vision and recklessness is deliberately left ambiguous.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Obama's 2006 book 'The Audacity of Hope' cemented the word in modern political rhetoric — but the phrase originally came from a sermon by Reverend Jeremiah Wright, who used it to describe the audacity of dreaming of a better world when the evidence suggests otherwise.

common_assets.image_prompt: Da Vinci style sepia sketch of a scene representing: His theory was a synthesis of Eastern philosophy and Western psychology.. The scene should capture the essence of 'Synthesis'. High quality, detailed, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: The combination of components or elements to form a connected whole.
en.example_sentence: His theory was a synthesis of Eastern philosophy and Western psychology.

hi.definition: Alag-alag tatvon ko milakar ek naya roop dena.
hi.example_sentence: Uska siddhant purvi darshan aur pashchimi manovigyan ka ek sanshleshan tha.

fr.definition: La combinaison de composants pour former un tout connecté.
fr.example_sentence: Sa théorie était une synthèse de la philosophie orientale et de la psychologie occidentale.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From Greek 'synthesis' (composition), from 'syn-' (together) + 'tithenai' (to place). Entered English in the 17th century via Chemistry and Logic.
english_deep_dive.trivia: In dialectics (Hegel/Marx), synthesis is the resolution of the conflict between a 'thesis' and an 'antithesis'.

common_assets.image_prompt: High-contrast Death Note anime style illustration of a scene inspired by a simple children's talent show: a cute singing dog wearing a shiny jacket stands on a small wooden stage with arms wide, while all the other little animals cheer wildly and throw flowers at his feet in pure adulation. 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Excessive admiration or praise.
en.example_sentence: The cute singing dog stood on stage, soaking in the adulation as all the little animals cheered wildly and threw flowers.

hi.definition: Hadh se jyada tareef ya prashansa.
hi.example_sentence: Pyare gaane wale kutte ne manch par sabhi chhote janwaron dvara phool phenkne aur zor se taliyan bajane ki ati-prashansa ka anand liya.

fr.definition: Admiration ou louange excessive.
fr.example_sentence: Le mignon chien chanteur se tenait sur scène, profitant de l'adulation tandis que les petits animaux l'acclamaient et jetaient des fleurs.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From the Latin 'adulatio' (flattery). Originally, it described a dog happily wagging its tail at its master. Today, it simply describes anyone receiving an overwhelming amount of praise and cheering, like a famous pop star on a big stage.

recent_references.title: The Adulation of the Anti-Hero
recent_references.author: The Atlantic
recent_references.context: A cultural essay analyzing how modern television audiences blur the line between being entertained by villainous protagonists and offering them genuine adulation, turning toxic traits into worship.

recent_references.title: When Fandom Crosses into Adulation
recent_references.author: The Guardian
recent_references.context: An exploration of modern pop music fanbases, arguing that healthy appreciation for artists has frequently mutated into blind adulation where no criticism is tolerated.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Even though it means huge praise now, the Latin root originally just meant a dog wagging its tail to get a treat!

common_assets.image_prompt: Dreamy Surrealist Salvador Dali style illustration of a scene inspired by a simple children's fable: a smart little owl sitting on a tree branch wearing tiny glasses, calmly holding up an open umbrella just before a single dark raincloud appears, while little woodland animals look up in amazement at his prescient planning. 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Having or showing knowledge of events before they take place.
en.example_sentence: The smart little owl opened his umbrella right before the rain started, shocking the other animals with his prescient planning.

hi.definition: Ghatnaon ke hone se pehle unka gyan hona.
hi.example_sentence: Samajhdar chhote ullu ne barish shuru hone se theek pehle apna chhata khol liya, jisse uski purv-gyani soch dekh sab hairaan reh gaye.

fr.definition: Avoir ou montrer la connaissance des événements avant qu'ils ne se produisent.
fr.example_sentence: Le petit hibou intelligent ouvrit son parapluie juste avant la pluie, surprenant les autres animaux par sa planification presciente.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From the Latin 'praescientia' (foreknowledge). It used to be tied to scary magical prophecy, but today it just describes someone who can accurately guess what will happen next—like checking the sky before a storm.

recent_references.title: The Prescient Warnings of Pandemic Fiction
recent_references.author: The New York Times
recent_references.context: A deep dive into how fiction writers mapped out societal reactions to global lockdowns years before they occurred, highlighting the prescient nature of science fiction.

recent_references.title: AI and the Myth of Prescience
recent_references.author: The Atlantic
recent_references.context: An essay arguing that we continuously mistake algorithmic pattern-matching for genuine prescience, confusing a machine's ability to calculate probabilities with the human intuition of truly seeing the future.

english_deep_dive.trivia: While its Latin root 'praescire' means simply 'to know beforehand', achieving true prescience is getting easier — as of April 2026, new AI models can even predict the weather with near-perfect accuracy weeks in advance!

common_assets.image_prompt: Photorealistic sci-fi style illustration of a scene inspired by a futuristic children's story: a sleek, high-tech cleaning robot aggressively sweeping a dusty futuristic room, but its own powerful exhaust vents are blowing massive clouds of dust back into the air, creating a paradox where cleaning makes it dirtier. 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: A seemingly absurd or self-contradictory statement or proposition that when investigated or explained may prove to be well founded or true.
en.example_sentence: The sleek robot tried to clean the room, but its exhaust blew dust everywhere, creating a funny paradox where cleaning made things dirtier!

hi.definition: Aisi baat jo dekhne mein virodhi lage par sach ho sakti hai.
hi.example_sentence: Aadhunik robot ne kamra saaf karne ki koshish ki, par uske dhuein ne itni dhool udayi ki kamra aur ganda ho gaya—kaisa virodhabhas!

fr.definition: Une déclaration apparemment contradictoire qui peut s'avérer vraie.
fr.example_sentence: Le robot élégant essayait de nettoyer la pièce, mais son échappement soufflait de la poussière partout, créant un drôle de paradoxe!

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From the Greek 'paradoxon', meaning 'contrary to expectation'. Today, it simply describes those funny, head-scratching situations where trying to fix a problem accidentally makes it worse or does the exact opposite.

recent_references.title: The Productivity Paradox: Why Hustle Culture Fails
recent_references.author: Forbes
recent_references.context: A simple piece exploring the modern office paradox where constantly working faster eventually causes so many burnout mistakes that it slows everything down.

recent_references.title: Helping Kids Understand Logic
recent_references.author: Scientific American
recent_references.context: An interesting look at how children begin to grasp complex contradictory scenarios around age seven using simple visual metaphors for paradoxes.

english_deep_dive.trivia: While the Greek philosophers loved debating them thousands of years ago, paradoxes remain fresh—recently, tech leaders noted an 'AI paradox' where smarter computer helpers sometimes make simple tasks take twice as long to finish!

common_assets.image_prompt: Soft Watercolor painting style illustration of a scene inspired by a simple children's detective story: a cute, furry bear detective with a magnifying glass is carefully examining tiny blue footprints that match his own fur, completely ignoring the giant, scary monster footprints right next to them. 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: The tendency to interpret new evidence as confirmation of one's existing beliefs or theories.
en.example_sentence: The cute detective bear only examined the tiny blue paw prints that matched his own fur, completely ignoring the giant monster tracks.

hi.definition: Nayi jaankari ko apne purane vishwas ko sahi saabit karne ke nazariye se dekhna.
hi.example_sentence: Jasoos bhalu ne sirf apne nile rang jaise nishanon ko dekha, aur bade danav ke pairon ke nishanon ko puri tarah andekha kar diya.

fr.definition: La tendance à interpréter de nouvelles preuves comme une confirmation de ses croyances existantes.
fr.example_sentence: Le mignon ours détective n'a examiné que les empreintes bleues correspondant à sa fourrure, ignorant complètement les énormes traces de monstre.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Coined by psychologist Peter Wason in the 1960s. It describes our brain's lazy habit of only paying attention to facts that prove us right, while throwing away anything that proves us wrong. Today, it perfectly explains why internet arguments never seem to end!

recent_references.title: The Psychology of Misinformation
recent_references.author: BBC News
recent_references.context: An insightful article explaining how social media algorithms feed into our confirmation bias by only showing us posts we already agree with.

recent_references.title: Why We Argue
recent_references.author: The Atlantic
recent_references.context: A deep dive into modern politics, showing how voters on all sides use confirmation bias to ignore flaws in their own favorite candidates.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Even highly trained scientists suffer from confirmation bias! To fight it, modern researchers use 'double-blind' studies, where even the scientists don't know who gets the real medicine until the very end, preventing them from accidentally seeing what they want to see.

common_assets.image_prompt: Soft Watercolor illustration style of a scene inspired by a modern political debate: a confident young politician stands at a wooden podium holding up a single, glowing red folder representing her most salient policy point, while hundreds of boring black-and-white papers flutter unnoticed to the floor around her. 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Most noticeable or important.
en.example_sentence: The young politician held up the glowing red folder, highlighting the most salient point of her entire campaign to the crowd.

hi.definition: Sabse mahatvapurn ya dhyan khinchne wala.
hi.example_sentence: Yuva neta ne chamakta laal folder upar uthakar apni campaign ka sabse mukhya bindu dikhaya, aur bheed ne taliyan bajayin.

fr.definition: Le plus notable ou important.
fr.example_sentence: La jeune politicienne a brandi le dossier rouge vif, soulignant le point le plus saillant de sa campagne devant la foule.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From the Latin 'salire', meaning 'to leap'. It originally described military lines jutting out into enemy territory. Over time, it evolved to describe any detail, fact, or argument that practically 'leaps' out at you because it is so important and noticeable.

recent_references.title: The Art of the Political Debate
recent_references.author: The Atlantic
recent_references.context: An analysis of how modern candidates succeed not by listing every policy, but by hammering home a few highly salient arguments.

recent_references.title: Information Overload in Elections
recent_references.author: BBC News
recent_references.context: A discussion on how voters filter out the noise and only focus on the most salient issues that directly affect their daily lives.

english_deep_dive.trivia: While it usually refers to important ideas today, 'salient' is still used in geography to describe a piece of land that noticeably sticks out into a body of water or another country's border!

common_assets.image_prompt: Studio Ghibli style illustration of a scene inspired by a classic fable: a lazy, chubby raccoon dressed like a prince lies on a velvet cushion, carelessly tossing endless piles of candy wrappers into his messy, falling-apart treehouse, living a completely dissolute lifestyle. 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Lax in morals; licentious.
en.example_sentence: The lazy raccoon prince lived a completely dissolute lifestyle, eating endless sweets and tossing wrappers everywhere while his treehouse fell apart.

hi.definition: Jiska charitra achha na ho; anaitik.
hi.example_sentence: Aalsi rajkumar racoon mithaiyan khakar aur har jagah kooda phenk kar ek poori tarah charitraheen jeevan ji raha tha.

fr.definition: Qui vit dans la débauche, sans retenue morale.
fr.example_sentence: Le prince raton laveur paresseux menait une vie complètement dissolue, mangeant des bonbons et jetant des emballages partout.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From the Latin 'dissolvere', meaning 'to loosen or dissolve'. While it originally meant something physically unfastened, it evolved to describe people who have 'loosened' all their self-control and discipline, lazily wasting away their time and responsibilities.

recent_references.title: The Fall of Modern Empires
recent_references.author: The Atlantic
recent_references.context: A historical analysis comparing the lazy, dissolute lifestyles of ancient emperors to out-of-touch modern billionaires who waste resources.

recent_references.title: Rethinking Work Ethic
recent_references.author: BBC News
recent_references.context: A cultural essay discussing how social media sometimes accidentally glorifies a dissolute, effort-free lifestyle to young teenagers.

english_deep_dive.trivia: Even though it sounds like 'dissolve', the two words actually share the exact same root! When you lead a dissolute life, you are basically letting your good habits 'dissolve' away.

common_assets.image_prompt: Moody Baroque oil painting style illustration of a scene inspired by a classic coming-of-age movie: a cheeky truant student wearing a bright red school backpack is gleefully playing a glowing arcade machine in a dimly lit room, while nervously glancing out the window at a yellow school bus driving away. Aspect ratio: 4:5

en.definition: A student who stays away from school without leave or explanation.
en.example_sentence: The cheeky truant student skipped school to play arcade games, nervously watching the yellow school bus drive away without him.

hi.definition: Wo vidyarthi jo bina bataye school se gayab rahe.
hi.example_sentence: Bhagode chhatra ne school chhodkar arcade game khela, aur ghabrakar peeli school bus ko wahan se jaate hue dekha.

fr.definition: Élève qui manque l'école sans permission.
fr.example_sentence: L'élève absentéiste a séché l'école pour jouer aux jeux d'arcade, regardant nerveusement le bus scolaire jaune s'éloigner.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: From the Old French 'truand', meaning a wandering beggar or rogue. While it originally described any lazy adult wandering the streets, by the 15th century, it narrowed to specifically mean a mischievous child skipping school to play all day.

recent_references.title: The Post-Pandemic Classroom
recent_references.author: The Atlantic
recent_references.context: An investigation into why schools are struggling with record numbers of truant students who have simply stopped attending classes.

recent_references.title: Why Kids Skip School
recent_references.author: The Guardian
recent_references.context: A cultural essay exploring the psychological reasons behind truant behavior in teenagers, arguing it often stems from anxiety rather than rebellion.

english_deep_dive.trivia: In the 16th century, 'playing truant' wasn't just a minor school rule—it was actually a criminal offense for wandering adults that could land them in serious legal trouble!

common_assets.image_prompt: Clean Disney Pixar 3D render style illustration of a scene inspired by a classic toy adventure movie: a lazy toy soldier with a fake thermometer in his mouth dramatically pretends to be sick in bed, while a strict plastic sergeant angrily points to the other soldiers marching outside, catching the malingerer red-handed. Aspect ratio: 4:5

en.definition: Someone who exaggerates or feigns illness in order to escape duty or work.
en.example_sentence: The strict plastic sergeant caught the malingerer red-handed, pointing at the lazy toy soldier pretending to be sick in bed.

hi.definition: Wo jo kaam se bachne ke liye bimar hone ka natak kare.
hi.example_sentence: Sakht sergeant ne bimar hone ka natak karte us kaamchor khilaune sipahi ko pakad liya, aur gusse se bahar ishara kiya.

fr.definition: Personne qui feint une maladie pour échapper à une corvée.
fr.example_sentence: Le sergent strict a surpris le simulateur, pointant du doigt le soldat jouet paresseux qui prétendait être malade dans son lit.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: Derived from the old French word 'malingre', meaning sickly or ailing. By the 18th century, the military adopted it to specifically describe lazy soldiers who feigned illness just to dodge their duties and avoid going into battle.

recent_references.title: The Rise of 'Quiet Quitting'

recent_references.context: A political analysis breaking down how candidates use highly specious statistics during debates to confidently trick voters.

recent_references.title: Debunking Diet Myths
recent_references.author: Health Magazine
recent_references.context: A medical review exposing how heavily marketed 'miracle' weight-loss pills rely entirely on completely specious scientific claims.

english_deep_dive.trivia: The word perfectly describes the sinister tactics of the pigs in George Orwell's famous 1945 novel 'Animal Farm', as they constantly use highly specious arguments to trick the other animals into giving up their rights!

common_assets.image_prompt: Warm Studio Ghibli style illustration of a highly comedic scene: a highly garrulous, eccentric old man sits on a park bench, passionately talking nonstop about his twenty cats while a young listener beside him desperately struggles to stay awake. Aspect ratio: 4:5

en.definition: Excessively talkative, especially on trivial or unimportant matters.
en.example_sentence: The highly garrulous old man sat on the park bench, talking nonstop about his cats while the young listener desperately struggled to stay awake.

hi.definition: Bekaar ki ya chhoti-chhoti baaton par bahut jyada bolne wala.
hi.example_sentence: Veh behad baatuni boodha aadmi park ki bench par baithkar lagatar apni billiyon ke baare mein bolta raha jabki uske bagal mein baitha yuva shrota jagne ke liye poori tarah sangharsh kar raha tha.

fr.definition: Excessivement bavard, surtout sur des sujets futiles ou peu importants.
fr.example_sentence: Le vieil homme très bavard était assis sur le banc du parc, parlant sans arrêt de ses chats tandis que le jeune auditeur luttait désespérément pour rester éveillé.

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: This expressive word comes from the Latin 'garrire', which literally means 'to chatter' or 'to prattle'. While 'loquacious' can describe someone who is eloquently talkative, 'garrulous' almost always describes someone who is annoyingly rambling about entirely trivial matters!

recent_references.title: Workplace Productivity
recent_references.author: Forbes
recent_references.context: A management article exploring how highly garrulous coworkers can unintentionally destroy office productivity by trapping colleagues in endless, trivial conversations.

recent_references.title: Modern Talk Shows
recent_references.author: Entertainment Weekly
recent_references.context: A media critique complaining that too many modern podcast hosts have become incredibly garrulous, talking for hours without actually saying anything meaningful.

english_deep_dive.trivia: The word perfectly captures the exhausting personality of Miss Bates in Jane Austen's famous 1815 novel 'Emma', as she is famously known for her hilariously garrulous, completely unfiltered, and endless rambling speeches!

common_assets.image_prompt: Bold Lino Cut graphic style. A joyous young woman sitting cross-legged on a literal massive, fluffy cumulus cloud floating high above a miniature city skyline. She is holding a glowing acceptance letter and laughing. The scene literalizes the idiom 'On cloud nine'. High contrast, detailed, 4:5 aspect ratio.

en.definition: Extremely happy and excited.
en.example_sentence: Clutching the acceptance letter from her dream university, she was on cloud nine, feeling as if she were floating high above the entire city.

hi.definition: Bahut jyada khush aur utsahi.
hi.example_sentence: Apne sapno ki university se acceptance letter milne ke baad, wo saatvein aasman par thi, jaise baadalon ke upar tair rahi ho.
hi.cultural_note: Direct conceptual translation, though Hindi literally references the 'seventh sky' instead of the 'ninth cloud'.

fr.definition: Extrêmement heureux et excité.
fr.example_sentence: Serrant la lettre d'acceptation de l'université de ses rêves, elle était sur un petit nuage, ayant l'impression de flotter loin au-dessus de la ville.
fr.cultural_note: Standard usage translating to 'on a little cloud'. Another common alternative is 'Être au septième ciel' (to be in seventh heaven).

english_deep_dive.evolution_story: The idiom's origin is widely attributed to the US Weather Bureau's 1950s classification of clouds, where 'Cloud Nine' (Cumulonimbus) represented the highest-rising cloud formation, reaching up to 40,000 feet. Being on 'cloud nine' literally meant being as high as one could possibly go. The phrase evolved into a metaphor for feeling elated or euphoric, eventually replacing earlier variations like 'cloud seven' in popular culture.

recent_references.title: Oracle on cloud nine, AI bets paying off
recent_references.author: The Economic Times
recent_references.context: Used in a financial journalism context to describe the tech company's soaring success following its investments in Artificial Intelligence [web:9].

recent_references.title: 'Chaarso paar' was BJP's fantasy: Shashi Tharoor
recent_references.author: Onmanorama
recent_references.context: News reports described Congress leader Shashi Tharoor as being 'on cloud nine' after securing a fourth consecutive victory from his Thiruvananthapuram Lok Sabha constituency [web:2].

english_deep_dive.trivia: While the meteorological origin is the most widely cited, another fascinating theory links the idiom to Buddhism, where 'Cloud 9' represents one of the highest stages of spiritual enlightenment. Furthermore, the phrase completely overshadowed 'cloud seven' (derived from Dante's 'Seventh Heaven') during the mid-20th century, making 'nine' the definitive number for ultimate happiness in modern English.
